title: The Extra Letters (Insertion / Frameshift)
learning_goal: Learn that inserting (or deleting) nucleotides that are NOT a multiple of 3 shifts the reading frame for every codon downstream of the change. This is a frameshift mutation - it can scramble the rest of the protein and even create a premature STOP codon, unlike the single-letter swaps in levels 2 and 3.
rules: You'll work with whichever gene you chose in Level 1 - your choice carries over automatically.
The reference DNA sequence is shown the whole time; the reference PROTEIN only appears once you re-translate the frameshifted sequence at the end.
Your tile pile has exactly 5 more tiles than the reference - a 5-nucleotide insertion.
You must find the insertion point yourself: guess a gap, and the game only tells you 'earlier' or 'later', never the exact spot.
Once located, guess the 5 inserted letters themselves in 'Vardle' - a Wordle-style guessing game using only A/C/G/T, with green/yellow/grey feedback per letter.
Once located, the reading frame is re-counted in groups of 3 from position 1 - it does NOT restart at the insertion.
A frameshift can create an early STOP codon, cutting the protein short before the reference protein's own stop.
Round 3 explains WHY the first codon is the true start - not because it's 'the first ATG', but because of what's upstream of it.
materials_provided: The reference nucleotide sequence (48 nt, ending in its STOP codon), shown on screen the whole time
A set of 53 nucleotide squares - the normal 48 reference tiles PLUS 5 extra nucleotide tiles mixed into the shuffled pile
A row of candidate insertion points (gaps between reference codons) for the player to test, rather than the exact location being revealed outright
Vardle: a Wordle-style guessing board for the 5 inserted letters, with an A/C/G/T letter picker and green/yellow/grey feedback per guess
instructions: 1. Compare your pile of 53 squares to the 48-nucleotide reference shown above - there are 5 extra tiles that don't belong to the reference gene.
2. Pick a candidate gap between two reference codons where you think the extra tiles were inserted, from the row of gap markers.
3. The game tells you if your guess is too early or too late in the sequence (like a number-guessing game), narrowing down the true insertion point without revealing it outright.

4. Once you land on the right gap, play Vardle to figure out the 5 inserted letters: submit a 5-letter A/C/G/T guess and read the color feedback (green = right letter, right spot; yellow = right letter, wrong spot; grey = not in the inserted sequence) to narrow it down.

5. Once solved, the reference and your full sample sequence are shown aligned, with a blank codon slot in the reference marking where the insertion sits - then re-split the WHOLE mutant sequence into new codons of 3, starting from position 1 again (the reading frame doesn't reset at the insertion - it just keeps reading in groups of 3 from the start).

6. Translate the new codons using the codon table. Notice that codons 1-5 still translate the same as the reference, but from codon 6 onward every codon - and every amino acid - is different, because the frame has shifted.

7. Watch for an early STOP codon - if one appears well before the reference protein's own stop, the protein gets cut short (truncated). The game explains: 'This is a frameshift mutation - because 5 is not a multiple of 3, everything after the insertion is read in the wrong groups of 3, scrambling the protein and often stopping it early.'

insertion_details.why_multiple_of_3_matters: Insertions/deletions of 3 (or a multiple of 3) nucleotides just add or remove whole codons (whole amino acids) and keep the reading frame intact. An indel of any other length, like 5 here, shifts every downstream codon boundary - a frameshift.

mutant.classification: Frameshift (insertion) mutation - codons 1-5 are unaffected, but codon 6 onward is completely different from the reference because the frame has shifted.

round3.title: Round 3 - Why THIS Methionine Is The Start
explanation: Every ATG codes for Methionine and reads as 'M' - but only ONE ATG per gene is actually used by the ribosome to START translation.

In bacteria, the ribosome doesn't hunt for 'the first ATG it sees'. A short upstream sequence called the Shine-Dalgarno (SD) sequence - purine-rich, commonly close to AGGAGG - base-pairs with the ribosome's rRNA and physically parks it a fixed short spacing before the real start codon.

Below is the REAL upstream GenBank sequence for this gene, right before codon 1 - not an invented example. Where a plausible SD-like motif is visible in that real sequence, it's highlighted; where the available fragment doesn't give us enough real upstream sequence to be sure, we say so instead of making one up.

Any OTHER ATG appearing later in the same reading frame (like the internal one you may have spotted) is just an internal Methionine amino acid in the middle of the protein - there's no SD sequence in front of it, so the ribosome never initiates there.
round3.quiz_question: Which codon number is the TRUE translation start for this gene?
round3.quiz_explanation: Codon 1 is correct - not because it's 'the first ATG', but because that's where the upstream ribosome-binding context (Shine-Dalgarno region) positions the ribosome. Any internal ATG is read as an internal Methionine only, once translation is already underway.

jlpA.source_note: Real upstream sequence from GenBank AF295104.1 (complete cds record - the true annotated ATG start is confirmed at this position, with 199 nt of real 5' context available; shown here is the 25 nt immediately before codon 1).
jlpA.confidence_note: This is a strong real example: AGGAG sits 6-11 nt before the start codon - right in the classic optimal spacing range - and closely matches the AGGAGG Shine-Dalgarno consensus. A genuinely convincing real ribosome-binding site.

cadF.source_note: Real upstream sequence from GenBank OR876352.1 (only 18 nt available in this partial-cds record - the clone starts very close to the gene itself, so this is ALL the upstream context that exists in this fragment).
cadF.confidence_note: There's a weak purine-rich candidate (GAAGG) about 10-15 nt before the start codon - plausible, but with only 18 nt of real upstream sequence to work with, we can't be fully confident this is the actual Shine-Dalgarno sequence rather than coincidence. More real upstream genome sequence likely exists beyond what this GenBank fragment includes.

ciaB.source_note: Real upstream sequence from GenBank AF114831.1 (complete cds record, so more 5' context is available).
ciaB.confidence_note: This one is a strong real example: AGGAGA sits 8-14 nt before the start codon and closely matches the classic AGGAGG Shine-Dalgarno consensus - a genuinely plausible ribosome-binding site in the real sequence.
